analogy: Think of "Soil" through the chapter's own opening images: Motion and Time 9 I n Class VI, you learnt about different types of motions. You learnt that a motion could be along a straight line, it could be circular or periodic. 9.1 SLOW OR FAST We know that some vehicles move faster than others.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 9.1 SLOW OR FAST, M OTION AND T IME 93, 9.2 S PEED, SCIENCE 94. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Soil is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that motion and Time 9 I n Class VI, you learnt about different types of motions.

The text explains that you learnt that a motion could be along a straight line, it could be circular or periodic.

The text explains that 9.1 SLOW OR FAST We know that some vehicles move faster than others.

The text explains that even the same vehicle may move faster or slower at different times.

The text explains that make a list of ten objects moving along a straight path.

The text explains that group the motion of these objects as slow and fast.

The text explains that how did you decide which object is moving slow and which one is moving fast?

The text explains that if vehicles are moving on a road in the same direction, we can easily tell which one of them is moving faster than the other.

The text explains that let us look at the motion of vehicles moving on a road.

The text explains that it shows the position of some vehicles moving on a road in the same direction at some instant of time.

The text explains that it shows the position of the same vehicles after some time.

The text explains that which one of them is moving the slowest of all?

The text explains that the distance moved by objects in a given interval of time can help us to decide which one is faster or slower.

The text explains that for example, imagine that you have gone to see off your friend at the bus stand.

The text explains that suppose you start pedalling your bicycle at the same time as the bus begins to Table 9.1 Some examples of different types of motion Example of Type of motion motion Along a straight line/circular/ periodic Soldiers in a march past Bullock cart moving on a straight road Hands of an athlete in a race Pedal of a bicycle in motion Motion of the Earth around the Sun Motion of a swing Motion of a pendulum It is common experience that the motion of some objects is slow while that of some others is fast.

The text explains that the distance covered by you after 5 minutes would be much smaller than that covered by the bus.

The text explains that 9.1 Vehicles moving in the same direction on a road 50 kilometres in one hour.

The text explains that a year was fixed as the time taken by the earth to complete one revolution of the sun.

The text explains that let your friend gently roll a ball along the ground in a direction perpendicular to the line.

The text explains that rockets, launching satellites into earth’s orbit, often attain speeds up to 8 km/s.

The text explains that can you calculate how fast is the rocket compared with the tortoise?

The text explains that 9.8 A bar graph showing runs scored by a team in each over interesting.

The text explains that in this chapter we shall consider only the positive values of quantities.

The text explains that let us try to understand this process with an example.

The text explains that cut out a triangular piece of a cardboard such that its one angle is equal to the latitude of your place and the angle opposite to it is a right angle.

The text explains that mark a line on the ground along the North-South direction.

The text explains that remember that the gnomon should always be placed in the North-South direction as shown in Fig.

The text explains that prepare a brief write up on each one of them.

The text explains that the write up may include the name of the device, the place of its origin, the period when it was used, the unit in which the time was measured by it and a drawing or a photograph of the device, if available.

The text explains that make a model of a sand clock which can measure a time interval of 2 minutes (Fig.

The text explains that you can perform an interesting activity when you visit a park to ride a swing.

The text explains that make the swing oscillate without anyone sitting on it.

The text explains that find its time period in the same way as you did for the pendulum.

The text explains that make sure that there are no jerks in the motion of the swing.

The text explains that repeat the activity with different persons sitting on the swing.

The text explains that compare the time period of the swing measured in different cases.

The text explains that what conclusions do you draw from this activity?

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 9: Soil

concise_notes.definition: 9.1 SLOW OR FAST We know that some vehicles move faster than others.
Motion and Time 9 I n Class VI, you learnt about different types of motions.
9.2 S PEED You are probably familiar with the word speed.
SCIENCE 94 We can determine the speed of a given object once we can measure the time taken by it to cover a certain distance.
9.3 M EASUREMENT OF TIME If you did not have a clock, how would you decide what time of the day it is?
Motion and Time 9 I n Class VI, you learnt about different types of motions.
SCIENCE 94 We can determine the speed of a given object once we can measure the time taken by it to cover a certain distance.
Motion and Time 9 I n Class VI, you learnt about different types of motions.
Clocks or watches are perhaps the most common time measuring devices.
SCIENCE 94 We can determine the speed of a given object once we can measure the time taken by it to cover a certain distance.

prelims_traps: Do not treat 9.1 SLOW OR FAST and M OTION AND T IME 93 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Soil; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
